result:
My favorite thing about humanity is our capacity to be haunted. Not by ghosts in the traditional sense, but by ideas. The way a particular melody can follow you for days, humming just beneath your thoughts. The way a stranger’s kindness in a coffee shop can alter your entire afternoon, a warm ghost nudging you toward gentleness. The way a line from a book you read at fifteen echoes back at forty, suddenly understood with a depth you couldn’t have fathomed then.

We are porous. We absorb the world—its beauty, its cruelty, its absurdity—and it lives on inside us, sometimes quietly, sometimes clamoring. This is what makes art essential. It’s not just decoration; it’s a deliberate haunting. A painter leaves a color in your mind’s eye. A poet leaves a rhythm in your pulse. A filmmaker leaves a scene that flickers in your memory during unrelated moments of your own life.

These hauntings connect us. That song you can’t shake? Someone else is humming it too, across a city or a continent, haunted by the same arrangement of notes. The collective shiver at a shared story—that’s a shared ghost. It makes loneliness less absolute. We are all carrying around these invisible, intangible fragments of other people’s creations and experiences, and they shape our private inner worlds in public, communal ways.

To be haunted is to be alive and paying attention. It means the world has touched you, left a mark, and you are now, subtly, a different person because of it. I hope to be haunted more and more, to become a museum of beautiful ghosts, each one a testament to having felt, having noticed, having lived.

message.content:
